text: Our next paper is actually going to be a video and it will be the superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy from Cedars-Sinai Medical Center presented by Dr. Alison Barrett. Good afternoon. Thank you for the privilege to present today. I'm going to be presenting a case report from Cedars-Sinai of a patient who developed superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy.
There are no disclosures from any of the participants.

The patient was a thirty nine year old female whose status post laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for a BMI of thirty six. She had a seventy pound weight loss at four months post operatively and developed unrelenting nausea, emesis and epigastric pain.

CT scan was performed which demonstrated narrowing of the duodenum between the aorta and the SMA which was consistent with a diagnosis of SMA syndrome. She then underwent mesenteric artery duplex which demonstrated a decreased angle between the aorta and SMA of eighteen degrees as well as a distance between the aorta and SMA of five point eight millimeters, both of which were consistent with the diagnosis. She also underwent an upper GI study which demonstrated a functional obstruction at the third portion of the duodenum.

SMA syndrome is duodenal obstruction between the aorta and the SMA. It's often precipitated by extreme weight loss. Symptoms are fairly nonspecific and can include postprandial fullness, nausea, and epigastric pain. And they're often relieved by positional changes such as sitting forward, lying prone, or in a left lateral decubitus position.

The diagnosis of SMA syndrome is by radiologic criteria, including a decreased angle between the aorta and the SMA of six to twenty-two degrees and a decreased distance of two to eight millimeters, both of which our patient had

Surgical options for the treatment of SMA syndrome include gastrojejunostomy, something called Strong's procedure which involves division of the ligament of trites and duodenal mobilization and a duodenal jejunostomy. In this particular patient we opted for a laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy which is most well described in the literature.
An endoscopic view in the operating room reveals narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum, visibly compressed between the pulsating superior mesenteric artery and the aorta.

The laparoscopic view shows the transverse colon reflected cranially, revealing the middle colic vessels and the takeoff of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The SMA is observed crossing the third portion of the duodenum, visible on the left side of the screen.

During simultaneous endoscopy, the surgeon identifies the narrowed section of the duodenum, which visually correlates with the superior mesenteric artery crossing the third portion of the duodenum. The endoscopic image on the left shows the distal narrowing of the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum and incises the transverse mesocolon to expose the duodenum. The lateral portion of the second part of the duodenum is opened, and the duodenum is fully mobilized. A limb of jejunum, approximately twenty centimeters distal to the ligament of trites, is brought up for a lateral side-to-side anastomosis at the most dependent portion of the second part of the duodenum.

The surgeon places a stay suture between the jejunum and the duodenum to orient the anastomosis. An enterotomy is created in both the duodenum and the jejunum, preparing for the passage of a linear stapler.

The surgeon places a white load, 45mm linear stapler at the anastomosis site between the jejunum and duodenum, ensuring careful positioning to avoid compromising the transverse mesocolon and the superior mesenteric artery, which are located near the distal tip of the stapler.

The surgeon fires the stapler, creating a staple line that is inspected and found to be hemostatic and widely patent. A hand-sewn two-layer technique is used to over-sew the defect, ensuring the anastomosis remains wide and unobstructed.

The surgeon places a stay suture inferiorly to orient the suture line, followed by closure of the common defect using two layers of vical suture. The second layer is applied with a lapar tie, completing the suture line.

The surgeon inspects the lateral side-to-side anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum, observing its wide and hemostatic appearance. An endoscopic view shows the third portion of the duodenum being narrowed between pulsatile vessels, with the anastomosis visible several centimeters proximal to this narrowing on the right.

The surgeon uses an endoscope to perform a leak test on the anastomosis by instilling methylene blue. The anastomosis appears wide, and the irrigation flows distally without any visible evidence of leakage.

So the patient did very well post-operatively and she began a liquid diet on day two and was discharged home on day five. That followed several months later, she had noted dramatic improvement in her symptoms. So in conclusion, SMA syndrome is a rare diagnosis which should be considered after extreme weight loss, especially in the bariatric population. A laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy is a technically feasible and a successful option for the management of SMA syndrome.
Thank you.

Questions? Let me begin by complimenting you on your use of intraoperative endoscopy. I think it was not only necessary for this case, but it's a very important thing. It took approximately one year for me to get the OR staff to simply go ahead and have flexible endoscope in the room for all four gut cases that were being done laparoscopically, but now it's routine. we had to build that culture.

One of the things where you use endoscopy early in your procedure that can occur is you can get excessive distension of the small bowel. Will you talk just a little bit about either your experience with that problem or what techniques you use to prevent that from happening? In this particular case, we had planned to do both intraoperatively, and so we accessed the abdomen laparoscopically and clamped off the distal bowel before instilling any insufflation with the endoscope. And then when we were completed with the first part of the procedure with the endoscope, we took out as much air as we could Do you ever use CO₂ instead of air? Yeah, we do. If we're doing something like this, we try to use CO₂ only. Very good. Thank you very much.
